content.full_text:
A-91-433(Martin)

The Section 85300 analysis in this letter is SUPERSEDED by the

1998 amendments to Regulation 18530. Regulation 18530 revised the

Commission's interpretation of Government Code Section 85300. Prior

to the amendment, the Commission interpreted Section 85300 to prohibit

the use of public resources for campaign purposes. Amended Regulation

18530 provides that Section 85300 only prohibits the payment of public

monies for the public financing of elections.

SUPERSEDED IN PART
BY 1998 AMENDMENTS TO
REGULATION 18530

California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 17, 1991


James Martin
100 Park Place, Suite 165
San Ramon, California 94583

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-91-423

Dear Mr. Martin:

This is in response to your request for written advice
concerning the mass mailing and other restrictions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Is it permissible for the Castro Valley Sanitary District
Board to send an "informational letter" at public expense to
ratepayers when the mailing mentions the "board" several times,
and three of the five board members are seeking reelection within
two or three weeks of the mailing?

CONCLUSION

The sanitary district board "informational letter" which
refers to the "board" in the body of the letter, and which will be
sent within two or three weeks of a district election does not
violate the mass mailing restrictions, nor does it constitute a
prohibited expense under the Act. Although the district
letterhead includes the names of the five board members,
references to the "board" in the text of the letter are not
references to individual board members.

FACTS

Board members of the Castro Valley Sanitary District voted on
September 3, 1991, to send out an "informational letter" to

_______________
1 Government Code Section 81000-91015. All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated. All
references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the
California Code of Regulations.

428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916) 322-5660

File No. A-91-423
Page 2


ratepayers of the district. It is anticipated that approximately
15,000 letters will be mailed, and the letter will be signed by
the district manager. The names of the board members will appear
only on the district letterhead, but the "board" body will be
referred several times. The "informational letter" bears upon
the charges being imposed upon the ratepayers in the district in
connection with its compliance with state waste stream diversion
mandates and a superior court ruling which invalidated a local
measure impacting such projects. The letter will be sent in
October, 1991, once the superior court ruling becomes a judgment.
Three of the five board members are seeking reelection in
November, 1991.

                              ANALYSIS

     You seek written advice as to whether the mailing at public
expense of an "informational letter" is prohibited under the Act.
Section 89001 prohibits mass mailings at public expense. The
Commission adopted Regulation 18901² to clarify which mailings
were permissible and which were prohibited under the Act.³

     Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing is prohibited
only if all of the following apply:

          (1)  Any item sent is delivered, by any means,
     to the recipient at his or her residence, place of
     employment or business, or post office box. For
     purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item
     delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item,
     such as a videotape, record, or button, or a
     written document.

               (2)  The item sent either:

                    (A)  Features an elected officer
          affiliated with the agency which produces or
          sends the mailing, or

                    (B)  Includes the name, office,
          photograph, or other reference to an elected
          officer affiliated with the agency which
          produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared
          or sent in cooperation, consultation,


_______________________________
     ² A revised version of Regulation 18901 was adopted by the
Commission in December 1989. Effective January 18, 1990, the
Commission began advising on the basis of the language of the new
regulation. A copy of the revised regulation has been enclosed.

     ³ The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid
the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in Watson
v. Fair Political Practices Com. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1059.

File No. A-91-423
Page 3


coordination, or concert with the elected
officer;

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is
paid for with public moneys; or

(B) Costs of design, production, and
printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public
moneys, and the design, production, or
printing is done with the intent of sending
the item other than as permitted by this
regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially
similar items are sent, in a single calendar month,
excluding any item sent in response to an
unsolicited request and any item described in
subdivision (b);

Regulation 18901(a).

A mass mailing consists of 200 or more substantially similar
pieces of mail sent in any one calendar month. (Section 82041.5,
Regulation 18901(d).) The "informational letter" you have
submitted for our review constitutes a mass mailing because you
plan to mail it to ratepayers of the sanitary district, at public
expense, in quantities greater than 200 pieces per calendar month.

Under the regulation, if an item does not include the name,
photograph or any reference to an elected officer who is
affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the
mailing, the item is not subject to the restrictions of the
regulation. (Regulation 18901(a)(2); Taormina Advice Letter,
No. I-90-634.) However, if the mailing has been prepared or sent
in coordination, coordination or concert with the
elected officer, any use of the elected officer's name, photograph
or office, or any reference to the elected officer is prohibited.
(Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B) emphasis added.)

On the basis of the facts you provided, the mailing of an
"informational letter" by the board is not restricted with respect
to references to the "board" as a body even though three board
members are seeking reelection in November, 1991. A general
reference to "board" is not a reference to individual board
members. (Owen Advice Letter, No. I-90-704.)

The materials you have provided indicate the "informational
letter" will be sent on district letterhead which includes the
names of all five board members and the district manager's name,
however. The letter will be signed by the manager. Regulation
18901(b)(1) provides exceptions to the mass mailing prohibitions
of Section 89001 which are applicable when an elected officer's
name appears only in the letterhead or logotype of the stationery
or envelopes of the agency sending the mailing, or of the elected

File No. A-91-423
Page 4


officer. (Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-89-088 and Wilcox Advice
Letter, No. A-89-141.)
18901 (b) provides:

     (b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a), mass
mailing of the following items is not prohibited by
Section 89001:

     (1) Any item in which the elected officer's name
appears only in the letterhead or logotype of the
stationery, forms (including "For Your Information"
or "Compliments of" cards), and envelopes of the
agency sending the mailing, or of a committee of
the agency, or of the elected officer, or in a
roster listing containing the names of all elected
officers of the agency. In any such item, the
names of all elected officers must appear in the
same type size, typeface, type color, and location.
Such item may not include the elected officer's
photograph, signature, or any other reference to
the elected officer, except as specifically
permitted in this subdivision (b)(1) or elsewhere
in this regulation.

                    Regulation 18901(b) (emphasis
                    added).

     The stationery on which the board's proposed mailing is
printed appears to comply with the provisions of Regulation
18901(b). Other than the listing on the upper left-hand side of
the letterhead, there appear to be no other references to
individual board members. Consequently, the mass mailing falls
within the exception in Regulation 18901(b)(1) and is not
prohibited. This exception, however, is limited to the inclusion
of the board members' name in the letterhead and not in the text
itself. Thus any reference or inference to the elected official
in the body of the document is prohibited unless another exception
applies. (Murphy Advice Letter, A-89-088.)

     Please note that a mailing disseminated for the purpose of
seeking office and paid for with public funds is prohibited under
Section 85300 of the Act. However, the information letter does
not advocate the reelection of the board members or refer to their
candidacy. Consequently, Section 85300 would also not prohibit

File No. A-91-423
Page 5


the mailing.³  (Calhoun Advice Letter, A-91-276.)

     I trust that the foregoing has adequately responded to your
request for advice as to the applicability of the mass mailing and
other restrictions of the Act.  If you have any questions, please
contact me at (916) 322-5901.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        Scott Hallabrin
                                        Acting General Counsel

                                        [illegible]
                                        By:  Luisa Menchaca
                                             Counsel, Legal Division

SH:LM:aa

Enclosures


_____________________________
     ³  Nothing in this letter should be construed to be an authoriza-
tion for any specific expenditures made by a state or local
government agency.  The Commission cannot determine whether an
expenditure is a legitimate government expenditure, we can only
advise as to whether such expenditures are a violation of the
provisions of the Act.  Moreover, other provisions of the law may
apply to these facts which are outside the jurisdiction of the
Commission.  (See, e.g. Penal Code Section 424; Stanson v. Mott
(1976) 17 Cal.3d 206; People v. Sperl (1976) 54 Cal.App.3d 640;
and People v. Battin (1978) 77 Cal.App.3d 635.)

sections.question: Is it permissible for the Castro Valley Sanitary District Board to send an "informational letter" at public expense to ratepayers when the mailing mentions the "board" several times, and three of the five board members are seeking reelection within two or three weeks of the mailing?
sections.conclusion: The sanitary district board "informational letter" which refers to the "board" in the body of the letter, and which will be sent within two or three weeks of a district election does not violate the mass mailing restrictions, nor does it constitute a prohibited public expense under the Act. Although the district letterhead includes the names of the five board members, references to the "board" in the text of the letter are not references to individual board members.

Board members of the Castro Valley Sanitary District voted on September 3, 1991, to send out an "informational letter" to

All references to regulations are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

ratepayers of the district. It is anticipated that approximately 15,000 letters will be mailed, and the letter will be signed by the district manager. The names of the board members will appear only on the district letterhead, but the "board" as a body will be referenced several times. The "informational letter" bears upon the charges being imposed upon the ratepayers in the district in connection with its compliance with state waste stream diversion mandates and a superior court ruling which invalidated a local measure impacting such projects. The letter will be sent in October, 1991, once the superior court ruling becomes a judgment. Three of the five board members are seeking reelection in November, 1991.
sections.analysis:
You seek written advice as to whether the mailing at public expense of an "informational letter" is prohibited under the Act. Section 89001 prohibits mass mailings at public expense. The Commission adopted Regulation 189012 to clarify which mailings were permissible and which were prohibited under the Act.3

Regulation 18901(a) provides that a mailing is prohibited only if all of the following apply:

(1) Any item sent is delivered, by any means, to the recipient at his or her residence, place of employment or business, or post office box. For purposes of this subdivision (a)(1), the item delivered to the recipient must be a tangible item, such as a videotape, record, or button, or a written document.

(2) The item sent either:

(A) Features an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, or

(B) Includes the name, office, photograph, or other reference to an elected officer affiliated with the agency which produces or sends the mailing, and is prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation,

2 A revised version of Regulation 18901 was adopted by the Commission in December 1989. Effective January 18, 1990, the Commission began advising on the basis of the language of the new regulation. A copy of the revised regulation has been enclosed.

3 The Commission's authority to interpret Section 89001 to avoid the absurd results of its literal application was upheld in Watson v. Fair Political Practices Com. (1990) 217 Cal.App.3d 1059.

coordination, or concert with the elected officer;

(3)(A) Any of the costs of distribution is paid for with public moneys; or

(B) Costs of design, production, and printing exceeding $50.00 are paid with public moneys, and the design, production, or printing is done with the intent of sending the item other than as permitted by this regulation.

(4) More than two hundred substantially similar items are sent, in a single calendar month, excluding any item sent in response to an unsolicited request and any item described in subdivision (b);

Regulation 18901(a).

A mass mailing consists of 200 or more substantially similar pieces of mail sent in any one calendar month. (Section 82041.5, Regulation 18901(d).) The "informational letter" you have submitted for our review constitutes a mass mailing because you plan to mail it to ratepayers of the sanitary district, at public expense, in quantities greater than 200 pieces per calendar month.

Under the regulation, if an item does not include the name, photograph or any reference to an elected officer who is affiliated with the agency that produces or distributes the mailing, the item is not subject to the restrictions of the regulation. (Regulation 18901(a)(2); Taormina Advice Letter, No. I-90-634.) However, if the mailing has been prepared or sent in cooperation, consultation, coordination or concert with the elected officer, any use of the elected officer's name, photograph or office, or any reference to the officer is prohibited. (Regulation 18901(a)(2)(B) emphasis added.)

On the basis of the facts you provided, the mailing of an "informational letter" by the board is not restricted with respect to references to the "board" as a body even though three board members are seeking reelection in November, 1991. A general reference to "board" is not a reference to individual board members. (Owen Advice Letter, No. I-90-704.)

The materials you have provided indicate the "informational letter" will be sent on district letterhead which includes the names of all five board members and the district manager's name, however. The letter will be signed by the manager. Regulation 18901(b)(1) provides exceptions to the mass mailing prohibitions of Section 89001 which are applicable when an elected officer's name appears only in the letterhead or logotype of the stationery or envelopes of the agency sending the mailing, or of the elected

officer. (Murphy Advice Letter, No. A-89-088 and Wilcox Advice Letter, No. A-89-141.)

18901 (b) provides:

(b) Notwithstanding subdivision (a), mass mailing of the following items is not prohibited by Section 89001:

(1) Any item in which the elected officer's name appears only in the letterhead or logotype of the stationery, forms (including "For Your Information" or "Compliments of" cards), and envelopes of the agency sending the mailing, or of a committee of the agency, or of the elected officer, or in a roster listing containing the names of all elected officers of the agency. In any such item, the names of all elected officers must appear in the same type size, typeface, type color, and location. Such item may not include the elected officer's photograph, signature, or any other reference to the elected officer, except as specifically permitted in this subdivision (b)(1) or elsewhere in this regulation.

Regulation 18901(b) (emphasis added).

The stationery on which the board's proposed mailing is printed appears to comply with the provisions of Regulation 18901(b). Other than the listing on the upper left-hand side of the letterhead, there appear to be no other references to individual board members. Consequently, the mass mailing falls within the exception in Regulation 18901(b)(1) and is not prohibited. This exception, however, is limited to the inclusion of the board members' name in the letterhead and not in the text itself. Thus any reference or inference to the elected official in the body of the document is prohibited unless another exception applies. (Murphy Advice Letter, A-89-088.)

Please note that a mailing disseminated for the purpose of seeking office and paid for with public funds is prohibited under Section 85300 of the Act. However, the informational letter does not advocate the reelection of the board members or refer to their candidacy. Consequently, Section 85300 would also not prohibit

the mailing.3 (Calhoun Advice Letter, A-91-276.)

I trust that the foregoing has adequately responded to your request for advice as to the applicability of the mass mailing and other restrictions of the Act. If you have any questions, please contact me at (916) 322-5901.

QUESTION: Is it permissible for the Castro Valley Sanitary District Board to send an "informational letter" at public expense to ratepayers when the mailing mentions the "board" several times, and three of the five board members are seeking reelection within two or three weeks of the mailing?

CONCLUSION: The sanitary district board "informational letter" which refers to the "board" in the body of the letter, and which will be sent within two or three weeks of a district election does not violate the mass mailing restrictions, nor does it constitute a prohibited public expense under the Act. Although the district letterhead includes the names of the five board members, references to the "board" in the text of the letter are not references to individual board members.
